comment_content:
Before midterm:

Propositional Logic, Predicate Logic, Inference, Sets and Functions, Counting, Combinations, Binomial, Inclusion-Exclusion, Probability, Conditional Probability, Random Variables

After midterm:

Cover the whole content(including the ones before midterm). There is an addition of modulo, gcds, cryptography(mostly about inverse mod, repeating squaring), algorithms, induction and recursion, and graph theory

That might sound a lot, but it's mostly on the basic understandings. If you're confident with your math background(example: great Math A Levels / Math Olympiad) then u should have no problem to take this course ASAP(I took it on my very first sem ;) ) and try to waive for it if needed
comment_teaching:
Papa has a really strong accent. But I like how Papa tries his best to teach, and even at times make his own helpful additional slides containing more practice problems or important concepts that might be ambiguous. Due to this, he usually falls behind Kenneth in terms of pacing. In fact, Kenneth's classes got 1 week of break in the very end, while Papa's class just got into the second half of graph theory, and uses the very final lecture for a review along with a past paper.

However, you can still access Papa's slides and watch his RVC recordings(well, this also apply to Kenneth's) for his lectures, so if you're already confident with Kenneth, you can still know the extra stuff in Papa's classes, and vice versa. I personally say that I prefer Papa due to his depth in teaching, and often times include some stuff from COMP 3711, and ofc he'll point those one out.
comment_workload:
There are In-Class Exersices like at the end of every lecture, except a few I guess. I would also say that Papa's In Class is quite fairly easy, and he even gives the major hints and then let us do the rest.

Inappropriate tip: if u think the content is too easy, u can just enter like.... idk 20 minutes before lecture ends, and then do the In-Class Exersice

There are 4 HWs, and each HW would take a fair amount of time, like 2 to 4 hours. Some open sources have also helped me for these questions.

Besides the lectures, in-class exs and HWs, there are also some other helpful resources, such as Kenneth's old lecture videos, tons of supplementary exersices(reviews for midterm & final, and extra problems on several topics), Papa's additional slides as I've mentioned before, tutorial problems, past papers, and several problems from TAs and UGTAs before the final exam.

So... that might sound a ton, but again the concept is quite shallow. So a bit of consistency in the problem solving should be enough

Additionally, I've actually grinded a ton of past papers, but it seems like the past paper is a bit harder than the actual midterm and final. So if u have limited time for this course, u can just trace back on the important concepts from lectures, HWs, tutorials, skim the past papers, and should be fine. Nonetheless, I'd also recommend the past papers for deeper understanding and problem solving of the content

Professor Kenneth Leung demonstrates a remarkable commitment to student engagement in
COMP2711 L2. Although the classroom is full, he pays careful attention to each individual to

dedication, allowing students the flexibility to learn more effectively.

comment_content: The content that is taught in COMP2711 is fundamental to computer science and provides a comprehensive spectrum of knowledge. It solidifies our foundation of mathematics and computer science and really trains our brain to function in a mathematical way.
comment_teaching: This lecturer is quite helpful as he actually teaches the content and explains the hard sections of the content. He would use whiteboard to draw diagrams to help us visualize the complex knowledge to ease it. Whenever we have questions, he would also answer the questions and address the needs of the students.
comment_workload: This course has quite a heavy workload as the materials are taught in a fast manner such that there is loads of self-study needed. Aside from the self study, there are mandatory in-class exercises to do in lectures. There is also homework every once a few weeks, but it is quite busy!
comment_grading: The professors are lenient on grading as some may have missed the first few exercises due to add/drop period, and they would allow late submissions and still count the scores. Other than that, the homeworks are also promptly graded, which is decent.

comment_content: The course content covers core knowledge points including logic and proof, sets, functions, algorithms, counting, induction, recursion, probability theory, and graph theory. In addition, the course introduces propositional logic and the use of truth tables, which strengthen students’ ability to reason and construct rigorous arguments. This course has weak relevance to calculus, and the logical thinking it cultivates is of great help to the development of problem-solving skills in subsequent programming learning.
comment_teaching: The instructor explains the concepts in detail, emphasizing both theoretical understanding and practical applications. The teaching pace is moderate, and in-class examples are provided to reinforce comprehension. However, the in-class exercises are sometimes repetitive, focusing more on basic skills rather than challenging applications. Moreover, the results of in-class exercises are counted toward the final grade.
comment_workload: Each class includes in-class exercises that must be submitted on the same day. These exercises help students consolidate what they have learned, but may pose some pressure for those with weaker mathematical backgrounds. The professor has mentioned that they will try to help such students “survive” the exams. [However, if you are not good at mathematics or have weak logical thinking skills, this course is definitely not as easy as you might imagine.]
comment_grading: The grading scheme is relatively clear, consisting of homework, quizzes, a midterm, and a final exam. The exam difficulty is moderate, primarily testing fundamental understanding and basic problem-solving skills rather than highly complex questions. Overall, students who keep up with the course should be able to achieve good grades.

comment_content:
Syllabus:

Pre-midterm:
Logic:
- Propositional logic
- Predicate logic
- Inference and proofs
Sets and functions:
- Injection, Surjection, Bijection
- Cardinality
Induction and recursion:
- Induction, strong induction, double induction, structural induction
- Recursive definition and step
Number Theory:
- Modular arithmetic
- GCDs and congruences (Chinese remainder theorem)
- Cryptography (Extended Euclidean algorithm, RSA)

Post-midterm:
Algorithms:
- Big Θ, big O, big Ω notations
Counting:
- Basic counting (Sum rule, product rule)
- Permutations and combinations
- Binomial coefficients (Pascal's identity)
- Inclusion-exclusion principle
Probability:
- Intro to probability (Expected value, variance)
- Conditional probability (Baye's theorem)
- Random variables (Binomial distribution, geometric distribution)
Graphs:
- Graphs (Bipartite, K, C)
P.S. order of topics may change from semester to semester, but the overall content doesn't change much.

This course teaches the fundamentals of logical reasoning and such, good and useful content.
Favours people who took M1/M2 in DSE (or just people with background in statistics/probability in general)
comment_teaching:
First half is taught by Yi Ke. Yi Ke's teaching makes already convoluted topics even harder to understand. You really gotta study hard for midterms because you can't rely on his teachings alone.

Second half is taught by Dr. James Tsz Him Cheung. James teaches way better than Yi Ke and the things he talks about actually makes sense. Also eager to help students after lectures end.

Arman is one of the TAs which is always fun, but tutorials don't count attendance so can be skipped.
comment_workload:
5 HWs with varying lengths, some HWs (*cough cough* induction) will take you forever to do, while others can be done within 2-3 hours at most.

Since the counting and probability taught are very fundamental, people with any background in statistics will find those topics extremely simple.
For people without background, a lot of extra work might be required to catch up to people who do.

For midterms and finals, not much can be done for revision other than doing past papers due to most questions being proof-based (other than probability and counting). Cheat sheets are only there to help you remember the sheer number of laws and theorems learnt throughout the course.
comment_grading:
Grading:
In-class quizzes: 10%
HW: 20% (5 HWs)
Midterm: 30% (1 A4 sized cheat sheet)
Finals: 40% (2 A4 sized cheat sheets)
Total: 100%

Technically, if you have friends in the same class, you can ask them to tell you when the quizzes are posted and do them remotely and skip lectures.

Finals:
- UQ: 71.25, Median: 59.5, Mean: 58.01, LQ: 44
- High: 96, Low: 12, SD: 17.92

Grading is fair imo, but you have to get 100%, or at least close to, for HWs and in-class quizzes due to the high averages (you can always chatgpt them).

Grades:

Grade: barely 90% (A+)

comment_workload: The workload was manageable and relatively light compared to other COMP courses. Weekly problem sets helped reinforce the key ideas, but they weren’t too time-consuming. comment_grading: Grading was very fair. Most of the exam questions were similar to tutorial problems or lecture examples, so there were no major surprises. If you stay consistent with the course, it’s quite possible to get a high grade without too much stress.

comment_content: COMP 2711 focuses on essential discrete mathematics concepts tailored for computer science applications, covering enumeration techniques, basic number theory, logic and proofs, recursion and recurrences, probability theory, and graph theory. Students will learn counting principles, permutations, and combinations alongside foundational number theory concepts like divisibility and modular arithmetic, crucial for algorithm design and cryptography. The course emphasizes logical reasoning through various proof techniques, including induction, and explores recursion with methods to solve recurrences relevant to algorithm efficiency. Additionally, it introduces probability theory, addressing random variables and distributions, as well as graph theory, encompassing graph algorithms for searching and optimization. Overall, the course equips students with the mathematical skills necessary for advanced topics in computer science and enhances their problem-solving abilities in a technology-driven landscape.

comment_teaching:
The teaching of this course is fabulous for me.
Lecture: Kenneth is good at teaching stuff like that. During the lecture, he can provide interesting examples to help us understand some concepts or theorems.
“I’m bigger than Desmond.” — Kenneth
He is very passionate about teaching and students feel engaged during his lectures. However, his pace is faster compared to other sections, and he sometimes directly skips some slides he deems not important or not difficult. So a brief preview before class is recommended.
Tutorial:
Tutorial is also engaging. This semester, Arman attended most of the tutorials. When he came, we played some interesting yet tricky games. Those who can solve the game can have small prizes. Besides that, the questions in tutorial notes are also well-crafted and suitable as an exercise.
Adding:
They have a telegram group chat where we can discuss exercise questions and share ideas with others, useful for assignments and revision.
comment_workload: Quite light workload considering it’s a comp course. Totally 4 homework sets, each of them can be done in 2-3 days. If you like skipping lectures, you’d better ask your friends to send the in-class exercise to you. If you aim for good grades, you need to spend more time in the exercise so that you can familiarize yourself with what formula/algorithm/rule to apply during exams.
comment_grading:
COMP course gives COMP grade— wide B range, but fierce competition for A range, so I may just leave a B here.
10% In-class exercise
25% HW
25% Midterm
40% Final
In-class exercise: It’s more like attendance since the exercise itself is very easy to deal with, and Kenneth even gives some hints so that everyone in the class can manage it. Only 80% of the exercises are counted for grade, which means you can still get the total 10% course grade if you miss 20% of the classes.
HW: totally 4 hw sets and each of them contains around 8 problems. You can spend 2-3 days(6-8 hrs) working through all the questions you can, then discuss the questions that you can’t solve in the group chat, TAs are very friendly and supportive to give hints. Assignments are made easier compared to previous semesters, with the mean of each set around 95.
Midterm: 2 hrs in total, containing around 8 questions. Every question is LQ, you need to provide all the steps of your solution. They will give you scores as long as your logic makes sense. The most challenging questions are about counting, combinatorial proofs. They made the questions easier compared to previous semesters, so the mean is kinda high—68, and SD is 20.
Final: 3 hrs in total, comprises around 10 questions. The format is similar to midterm. The most challenging questions are about probability, modular arithmetic, and algorithms. Final is also easier compared to previous semesters—mean is 70 and SD is 19.
me: total is around 98%, I will change “grading” into E if I don’t get A+. I’m not here to flex and I genuinely hope you guys can all do better than me, so good luck.

COMP2711 is a required course for DSCT/COMP students. It focuses on a wide range of concepts, but not in-depth, related to discrete mathematical tools in computer science.

If you have taken MATH 2411, you might find this course to be relatively easy.

However, taking this course in the summer term might not be a good idea, as the time is insufficient for revision. We end class on Friday, but we have the final exam the next day, which is Saturday.
There are around 600 slides waiting for you.

Most of the workload comes from completing past papers. You will need to spend about three hours on each past paper after finishing a day of lectures, which lasts three and a half hours.

comment_content:
The course covers the same topics as in a regular semester (they didn't even skip graph theory like they did in the spring semester). But the homeworks and exams are extremely easy compared to that of normal semester. So it might be less preferable to someone who prefers rigor. But I assume those guys will take 2711H anyways.
Although there are many topics, they are very introductory and don't go much in depth (though the questions can be quite challenging), so you would likely need to take other courses such as MATH 4141, MATH 3121, and MATH 2421 to gain a deeper understanding. I believe there is significant overlap with MATH 2421, so if you are a DSCT, COSC, or just a student interested in these subjects, you should definitely consider taking them together.

One interesting finding: The syllabus is an exact copy of UC Berkeley's Math 55 https://math.berkeley.edu/courses/overview/lowerdivcourses/math55
This is actually a good thing since UCB is known for being a strong CS school, and I don't mind because I don't have to pay $60,000(USD) per year and can still learn the same content.
comment_teaching:
On the first day of lecture, I remember Professor Tai saying, "I have taught this course 10 times," so my expectations were quite high. Unfortunately, they fell short. She struggles with pacing her lectures, frequently stumbles over words, and as a result, might skip crucial parts if she runs out of time or takes an extra 10 minutes to finish.

If it weren’t for the in-class quizzes, I would have only attended the tutorials. In fact, I highly recommend attending all tutorials led by Arman. He is clearly passionate about the subject, provides key insights, and emphasizes intuition in his explanations. He might also drop occasional hints about questions that could appear on exams (as he did for the midterms) during the tutorials.
comment_workload: I believe the workload in the summer semester is lighter, as the homework, midterms, and finals are much easier. So, it really depends on the grade you’re aiming for.
comment_grading:
Grading Scheme:

    In-class Quizzes: 18%
    Homework: 12%
    Mid-term Exam: 25%
    Final Exam: 45%

I am writing this review just after taking my final exam, so the final results haven't been released yet (I’ll update this later if I remember). There will be some exchange students also taking this course, so the mean might not accurately reflect the true UST average. Still, it’s quite competitive to score above +1 SD, as indicated by the high standard deviation and self-selection bias (students who choose to take a course during summer vacation may be more motivated than the general population, but that's just my guess).

Midterms:

    Mean: 60.84
    Median: 61
    High: 99
    Upper Quartile: 82.5
    Low: 0
    Lower Quartile: 41

While these scores might seem high compared to a normal semester, the questions were also significantly easier. The true UST mean (if you exclude exchange students) might have been 65+.

Note: I don't hold any prejudice against exchange students; they often spend less time studying and don't stress about grades as much as UST students, since they want to explore Hong Kong.

For this semester, more weight was given to quizzes, but the way the professor conducted them was less than satisfactory. For instance, she would allocate only 3 minutes for some questions that clearly required 5+ minutes of calculations. Additionally, some students skipped lectures and relied on friends to notify them when quizzes would be held. I noticed this because one time there were only 38 students in the class, yet 44 respondents after the quiz ended (simple pigeonhole principle). Given how quickly some students submitted their quizzes and got 100, I suspect they used ChatGPT, which is clearly unfair since quizzes are worth 18% of the grade.

comment_content:
I may be biased as I was taught under Mr. Leung's teaching, but the content was not too difficult. The topics taught were:
Logic, Combinations, Probability, Number Theory, Algorithms, Graphs
In terms of difficulty, (in my opinion) they would be arranged (from easiest to hardest)
Logic < Graphs < Algorithms < Number Theory < Combinations < Probability
The topics taught in:
Logic-- are relatively easy, just make sure not to make careless mistakes for these questions
Graphs-- are mostly "proof" questions. They won't ask you much about graph theory as it will be discussed more in year 3
Algorithms-- understand clearly what big O,theta, and omega are, and you're good to go
Number Theory questions are usually about decrypting texts and stuff like that, which are things that you can memorise. So these questions are practically free marks (unless you're careless)
Combinations and Probability-- now these questions are always tricky. A particular TA named Arman always creates creative and tricky questions. You cannot do these questions through sheer memorisation. You have to use your brain and aimlessly think of methods, which is why it is the hardest.

Comp2711 has a telegram group where you can ask questions to the TAs, and usually get an instant response, which I love.
comment_teaching:
I find Kenneth Leung's teachings to be very interesting. He is always very happy when he discusses the topics at hand. He makes these very simple analogies on certain complex topics to make us better understand them. I believe his teaching style is excellent, clear to understand as well as entertaining.

The TAs were all very helpful, especially Arman. Arman, from my experience, replies a lot more in the telegram group than other TAs. He sometimes sends custom questions to the group as extra practice questions for the exam. These custom questions may be hints to certain questions in the exam, so make sure to pay attention to what Arman writes. But Arman is also infamous for making hard questions in exams so :thumbs_up:
comment_workload:
In terms of the frequency of assignments, it is not a lot. There are only 6 homework to hand in. However, most of the time, the homework would usually take 1 week to finish. I would often find myself doing the homework overnight (like a proper UST student). But I wouldn't say it is "stressful" as there are only 6 homework to hand in.

It's good to keep in mind that the "complexity" of the homework questions is MUCH harder than those in the exams. A lot of us were very scared by the midterm because we thought the midterm questions were as hard as the homework ones, but they weren't really.

comment_grading:
In-class Quizzes: 10%
Homework 20%
Mid-term Exam 25%
Final Exam 45%
In-class quizzes will be conducted by Canvas Quizzes.  The 20% lowest scores (including no submissions)  will be dropped in calculating your final quiz score.  Quizzes conducted during the first two weeks (add/drop period) will not be counted.

There will be 6 homework assignments throughout the course.  The lowest score (including no submission) will be dropped in calculating your final homework score.

Homework late Policy: 1 point for every hour late.

For the midterm and final exam, you will be allowed to bring one (midterm) or two (final) A4-sized cheat sheets. It can be either single-sided or double-sided, handwritten or printed.

The exams may have optional bonus questions that are more challenging than the normal questions. The final letter grades will be first assigned without counting these bonus questions.  Then the bonus questions will be added to your final score for possible grade promotion (at most one sub-grade).

comment_content:
I think most reviews just offered info based on the course itself, without any context of the author, so I hope to briefly mention my background. Local student with math/M1 5**, 1013 and 1014 A, 2411 A-, not a big fan or talented in maths.

First half (before midterm):
Logic
Set and functions
Number theory
Algorithms

These topics are almost completely new to me, but some of them are quite interesting and thought-provoking. If I need to arrange them by increasing difficulty, it should be like this:
Algorithm<logic<Set and functions~Number theory.

Algorithm: basically just intro to algo, you just need to apply some knowledge from 1014, e.g. n!>n^2
Logic: just manipulating logical gates and inference questions
Set and functions: the questions to prove sets are countable/uncountable or having bijections are hard, you cannot practise a method to do the questions like the above 2 topics, you need to build up a mindset to do so.
Number theory: gcd and crypto, crypto questions could be practised, gcd questions could vary a bit and sometimes hard to deal with

Second half (after midterm):
Induction and recursion
Counting
Probability
Graphs

As a M1 students, the second half are easier to handle, especially counting and probability, difficulty is as follows:
Probability<Counting<Graphs<Induction and recursion

Probability and counting: mostly M1 stuffs with some new concepts, pigeonhole principle, derangement, but not really hard
Graphs: mostly definitions and questions are quite standard
Induction and recursion: personally think they are related but their difficulty should be ranked differently, recursion questions are much more harder than induction.

To all hkers:
You might think that my background is quite impressive but bear in mind there are lots of nds who have encountered most topics in high school. I am not particularly talented in maths but there are lots of resources for you to practise and you should try your best to finish them in the following order:

1. Past papers
2. Book exercise (supplementary exercise)
3. Book exercise (by sub-chapter)

I can promise you if the % of past papers and supplementary exercise you finish could reflect your final grade, if not equal to.

He also explains concepts quite clearly and he answers questions patiently after class/via email. But for some math concepts, he might assume they are basic/intuitive and just quickly went through. If you find you cannot understand the lecture content fully, you should find different ways to learn yourself. Here are some methods I used to improve my learning (arranged based on my preference):
1. rewatch the lecture recordings
2. youtube
3. read math/stack exchange posts
4. email prof/ask ta

comment_teaching:
Kenneth is a great teacher! Super enthusiastic during class and explains concepts clearly. I really enjoyed attending lectures and I think this was the only course in Fall for which I had 100% attendance.

Also happened to be a common core; it was a pleasure to have had the chance to nominate Kenneth for the Teaching Excellence Award.

This might not be the case for all semesters but Kenneth created a telegram group which became insanely active ~2 weeks before the finals due to people discussing questions back and forth. Although I have to say performance in the final exam hugely depends on the amount of personal studying and past paper practice you do, the community this group provided definitely helped clear some things like some algorithm questions.

Kenneth also gamified practice for the final exam. He went out of his way to create a website where students can create their own problems and exchange them with other students' (1 problem you create for 20 problems by other students). I only did 2-3 problems but due to the ample background most students taking this course had, they really aided my revision and also served as fun challenges!

Definitely take this course even if you're not a CS student :)

Revision for finals and midterms >> PAST PAPERS! Do as many as you can and you should get a decent grade!

comment_content:
In order:
Logic (Propositional Logic, Predicate Logic, Basic Proof Templates, etc.)
Sets and Functions (Naive Set Theory, Cardinality, etc.)
Counting (Permutations/Combinations, Pigeonhole Principle, Inclusion-Exclusion, Binomial Theorem, etc.)
Probability (Bayesian, Expectation/Variance, etc.)
Number Theory (Modulo, GCD, Cryptography)
Induction and Recursion (Weak/Strong/Structural Induction, Recurrence Relations, etc.)
Algorithms and Complexity (Big O, worst-case analysis, etc.)

For me, most of the concepts were familiar enough since I learnt of them in secondary school, but I could see some students struggling with some of them in terms of computation, especially Probability and Counting. All in all, I think the syllabus provides a solid mathematical foundation in Computer Science.
comment_teaching:
Prof. Leung was an overall great instructor! He always motivated students to answer questions and ask questions in class, and used many concrete examples to explain abstract concepts. The visuals he used to explain worst-case analysis, for example, has really helped me understand. The Zoom polls let us check our understanding and Leung was always ready to clear up misconceptions as represented in the poll results.

The Telegram group he and other TAs had opened have created an open, supportive and collaborative atmosphere for us to ask questions and support one another.
comment_workload:
Zoom polling: very very simple, if you pay attention to class you should easily get a full mark.

Homework: the homework is not too difficult as they employ the concepts in the notes fairly simply (aside from one or two questions). I spent an estimate of 1-2 hrs doing the Qs and another hour typesetting in LaTeX (NOT required but I like keeping things neat).

Midterm: Difficulty is just about right, lost 10+ points only because I misinterpreted the entire question about probability ;-;

Final: Simpler than I had anticipated, all concepts are tested fairly simply and separately, only difficult part was algorithmic analysis where you have to write out algorithms having the right big O with pseudocode.

All in all, for both exams, only hurdle to A range is to practice more with past paper problems and supplementary ones the TAs had generously provided.

While her English is acceptable, the lectures are very boring and the lecture notes are often incomplete, skipping steps and making the notes very difficult to understand. Concepts are not described clearly and the examples used are far too basic for you to understand what is being asked, some types of questions will not even have examples for you to refer to, meaning you have to Google on your own. And the poor teaching quality is reflected in the exam grades as the mean for my session is significantly lower than the other 2 sessions.

However, since Zoom polls are part of the course, you must attend these lectures and waste 80 minutes of your life sitting there when watching simple Youtube videos on your own makes learning this course far easier.

Tutorials do not require attendance, and all the TAs do is just repeating the answers on the tutorial questions, which you can just do yourself.
comment_workload:
For a 4 credit course it's quite good.

Homework don't take too much time to complete (some questions have full answers online), most of the time is just spent trying to understand the course materials due to the poor teaching quality. However since there are no big projects or assignments, workload is relatively low, with a good lecturer that explains well the workload for this course will be similar to 3 credit MATH course.

As for exams most of the time is spent doing past papers, which the course does provide graciously, doing them is very important as you get to try coming up with solutions to answers yourself, which you don't get to do at all otherwise.
comment_grading:
25% Homework: 6 homeworks, only take the score for the best 5. Quite difficult due to the poor teaching quality, luckily some questions have full answers online. Spend time doublechecking your work with your friends to make sure you got the correct answer since its such a big part of the course.

10% Zoom polls: 20 zoom polls, if you answer 10 correctly you get the full 10%. Some luck is required since its unlikely you will understand the lecture enough to answer questions during the same lecture. Mean for this is unknown but it's very likely that most people got all 10%.

25% Midterm: 5 questions, difficulty luckily is lower than the homework questions, but still high. Mean: 29/50

40% Finals: 8 questions, strong emphasis on the materials taught after the midterm, memorizing the way to do the RSA/gcd/mod questions is key because they are very mechanical. Mean: 40.6/80

Ended up with 2% above mean, and got a B, better grading than expected!
